perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Inform ation” means any and all confidential and/or proprietary information disclosed by the Casino t o the Receiving Party, which may in clude without limitation: financial information, business information, m arketing d ata, trade secrets, and other data, materials, products, plans, specifications, reports, manuals, computer software or programs, cont ractual relationships and other similar information delivered in any form or media.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means information the Casino designates as confidential in writing at the time of disclosure. The Casino will provide notice to the Receiving Party regarding what information is considered confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of 'Confidential Information' to require written designation at the time of disclosure and providing notice, we create a contradiction. The original definition encompassed a broad range of information, whereas the altered definition narrows it significantly and adds a procedural requirement. This contradicts section 2 where it implies confidential information is already identifiable and readily disclosed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information is disclosed to Receiving Party sol ely for the purpose of providing services for the Project. Receiving Party agrees to hold such Confidential Information in strict c onfidence. Receiving Party agrees not to use, divulge , reproduce or othe rwise make available the Confidential Information other than for the purposes of the Project and to disclose it only to its officers, agents, employees, partners, and professional advisors as ne cessary , who shall also be bound to comply with this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly. The changed text narrows the definition to only information designated as confidential in writing at the time of disclosure. Section 2 refers to 'Confidential Information' as if it is readily identifiable and implicitly assumes it's disclosed without prior written designation. The narrowed definition in the changed text therefore contradicts the implicit assumption in Section 2, creating an in-text contradiction. The change makes it unclear which information is actually considered confidential, rendering Section 2's obligations ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Upon the request of Casino, Receiving Party shall immediately return all Confidential Information received in written or tangible form, including all notes, copies, o r media containing such Confidential Information. Alternatively, Casino may direct R eceiving Party to destroy such Confidential Information, in which case Receiving Party shall confirm such destruction in writing within twenty (20) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Upon written request from the Casino, Receiving Party may return Confidential Information. The Receiving Party's decision to return Confidential Information is at their sole discretion. The Casino may request the Receiving Party to destroy such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the immediate return of confidential information upon request. The modified clause introduces optionality ('may return') and sole discretion for the Receiving Party, contradicting the binding nature implied in the original agreement. This contradicts section 3, which states that Receiving Party must protect the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, by giving the sole discretion to the receiving party to return the confidential information
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Receiving Party s hall protect the Confidentia l Information with at leas t t he same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no case, less than reasonable care.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces optionality ('may return') and sole discretion for the Receiving Party, whereas the original text mandated immediate return, creating a clear contradiction with Section 3. Section 3 requires the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care they use for their own, implying a responsibility to safeguard it. Granting the Receiving Party sole discretion to return the information directly contradicts this obligation, as they might choose not to return it, potentially compromising its protection, breaking compatibility with the contradicted section, and adherring to the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any other person or entity other than as provided herein, without Casino’s prior written cons ent. Neither party nor any of its representatives shall contact, either directly or indirectly, any governmental official, lender or other third party (other than a repre sentative as provided herein) to discuss the Project or the business or the assets of the other party, or a potential business transaction with or c oncerning the other party, without first obtaining the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Receiving Party shall make reasonable efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information to any other person or entity other than as provided herein. Each party may contact governmental officials regarding the Project.
perturbation.explanation: Originally, the Receiving Party was prohibited from disclosing Confidential Information without prior written consent. The modified clause replaces this prohibition with 'reasonable efforts,' significantly weakening the protection. It also allows both parties to contact government officials, contradicting the need for consent regarding project discussions. The weakening of the Confidential Information also creates a contradiction in section 11, where it says that a breach of this agreement may cause irreparable damage to the Casino
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. The parties agree t hat a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party may cause irreparable damage to the Casino and hereby agree that Casino will be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited disclosure of confidential information and restricted contact with governmental officials. The changed text weakens the protection of confidential information by changing the requirement to 'reasonable efforts' and allows contact with governmental officials. This directly contradicts section 11, which states that a breach of the agreement may cause irreparable damage to the Casino and allows the Casino to seek injunctive relief. The weakened confidentiality and permission to contact officials creates a strong contradiction because the agreement previously ensured the confidential information would not be exposed, which is now undermined.

perturbation.original_text: 10. T his A greement shall ha ve a term of three (3) years. Receiving P arty’s obligations in relation to technical and personally- identifiable inf ormation shall be inde finite.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall have a term of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause which states that 'Receiving Party’s obligations in relation to technical and personally- identifiable information shall be indefinite,' and only including that the agreement will last one year, we create a contradiction, given that the agreement will have a specific end date and information will no longer be able to be disclosed. Given that the agreement is shorter, it now contradicts the fact that Casino will be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. The parties agree t hat a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party may cause irreparable damage to the Casino and hereby agree that Casino will be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the agreement lasts three years and the Receiving Party's obligations regarding certain information are indefinite. The changed text shortens the agreement to one year and omits the indefinite obligation. This creates a direct contradiction with clause 11, as it is inconsistent to seek injunctive relief for a breach of an agreement that has a very limited duration. This is because the original agreement stated the obligations would be indefinite, and with the change, there is no more indefinite obligation.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Casino and Re ceiving Party have entered into discussions to _______________________________________________ ( herein after referred to as the “Project”) and, in the course of these discussions, Re ceiving Party will have access to Confidential Information (as defined below) about the Casino; and WHER EAS, the parties wish to protect such Confidential Inform ation.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Casino and Re ceiving Party may enter into discussions to _______________________________________________ ( herein after referred to as the “Project”).
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'have entered' to 'may enter', the agreement now suggests that the project and access to confidential information are not definite. This contradicts section 2, which says that 'Confidential Information is disclosed to Receiving Party sol ely for the purpose of providing services for the Project'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information is disclosed to Receiving Party sol ely for the purpose of providing services for the Project. Receiving Party agrees to hold such Confidential Information in strict c onfidence. Receiving Party agrees not to use, divulge , reproduce or othe rwise make available the Confidential Information other than for the purposes of the Project and to disclose it only to its officers, agents, employees, partners, and professional advisors as ne cessary , who shall also be bound to comply with this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'have entered' to 'may enter' introduces uncertainty about whether the project and access to confidential information are definite. This directly contradicts Section 2, which states that 'Confidential Information is disclosed to Receiving Party solely for the purpose of providing services for the Project.' The change suggests the project might not exist, negating the purpose of disclosing confidential information as described in Section 2. This is a strong contradiction as it makes the purpose of disclosure in Section 2 conditional on a potentially non-existent project.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Receiving Party shall have no obligations unde r this Agreement with respect to Conf idential Information if it can demonstrate tha t the information: ( a) is publicly av ailable at the time of disclosure; (b) be comes publ icly a vailable after di sclosure through no act of the receiving pa rty or its repr esentatives; (c) is in the receiving party’s possession free of obligation; (d)is deve loped by the receiving party without use of the Confidential Information or through other breach of this Agreement; ( e ) is disclosed to the receiving party by a third party without obligation; (e) is not identifiable a s confidential; o r ( f) is required to be publ icly disclosed pursuant to a properly executed subpoena or other regulatory or court order (“Order”), provided the receiving party ( i ) gives r easonable w ritten not ice to the disclosing pa rty, ( ii) gives the disclosing party a reasonable oppor tunity to respond to the Order, and (iii) limits dis closure to tha t portion of the Confidential Information re quired b y the Order.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Receiving Party shall have no obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information if it is required to be publicly disclosed pursuant to a properly executed subpoena or other regulatory or court order (“Order”), provided the receiving party ( i ) gives reasonable written notice to the disclosing party, ( ii) gives the disclosing party a reasonable opportunity to respond to the Order, and (iii) limits disclosure to that portion of the Confidential Information required by the Order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing exemptions (a) through (e) from Section 5, the agreement now contradicts Section 3, which mandates reasonable care in protecting confidential information. The receiving party is now obligated to protect information that is publicly available, developed independently, or received from third parties, which is illogical. This creates a legal inconsistency and enforceability issues regarding the scope of confidential information protection.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Receiving Party s hall protect the Confidentia l Information with at leas t t he same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no case, less than reasonable care.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes exemptions from the Receiving Party's obligations regarding Confidential Information. This directly contradicts Section 3, which requires the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information with a certain degree of care. The original text exempted information that was publicly available, developed independently, or received from third parties. By removing these exemptions, the changed text now requires the Receiving Party to protect information that it previously had no obligation to protect, creating a direct conflict between the obligations in Section 3 and the altered Section 5.
